sentences.text: My name is Hy Abrams.
I used to be Herschel Abrahamovits.
The English spelling of Hy Abrams is H-Y, A-B-R-A-M-S.
And Herschel Abrahamovits is H-E-R-S-H-E-L A-B-R-A-H-A-M-O-V-I-T-S.
I was born on August 18-- August 15, 1924, in Novoselica.
N-O-V-O-S-E-L-I-C-E.
It used to be Czechoslovakia.
And it's now the Ukraine.
My father's name was Mendel, Mendel Israel, M-E-N-D-E-L, Israel, I-S-R-A-E-L.
My mother's name was Channa Etya, C-H-A-N-A E-T-E.
Her maiden name was Fox, F-O-X.
We were with three sisters and one brother.
My oldest sister's name was Chia.
Then came Golda.
Then I was.
Then came Sarah.
And my younger brother was Chaim.
I was the middle one.
Well, it's like any brothers and sisters.
We used to love each other, and we used to fight, also.
It was a normal-- it was a normal thing.
It was a-- there was no such a thing as you are better and I am worse.
But we had difficulties between us, like children do.
Well, I'll be honest with you.
As far as I remember, I don't think we had any time to play.
Because around 6 o'clock in the morning, I have to go in cheder. 8 o'clock, I went home.
I had breakfast.
Around 9 o'clock, I have to go in public school till about 2 o'clock in the afternoon.
Then I came home.
We had a little to eat.
Then I had to go back in cheder.
And I used to be in cheder till about 7, 8 o'clock at night.
So you see, there wasn't much time to play.
No.
They-- no.
No.
They-- they usually-- the girls, in our town-- it was a small town.
And the girls in our town, it was important for them to learn, as far as Yiddish is concerned-- of course, we spoke-- our mother language was Yiddish.
But to write and read, that, the girls had to learn, because, usually, they had to go in shul and Shabbos and so on, when they got older.
So they had to learn how to read and write Yiddish.
But the boys were different.
We had to go in cheder.
And after that, most likely, in the Yeshiva, we had to learn more.
My father, if I remember, was in a-- we had a very-- we had a very small store, like you know, in any small town, where you could buy almost anything and everything.
But I guess it didn't go too well with the store.
He had to give it up.
Then he started to look-- work as a salesman.
He went to Germany.
He was in Germany.
He was in Belgium.
He was in England, you know, in all of those.
Mostly, he used to come home, he used to come home for the holidays.
Mostly for the High Holidays, he used to come home.
So basically, we were more with our mother than with our father.
But I do remember my father.
He-- any time when he was home, his most fascinating thing used to be to read.
He used to love to read papers, a book.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even-- even my mother used to complain on him, why he's reading all the time?
He says, this is what I love.
I guess I-- it's contagious.
I'm the same way.
And what was your town like?
Our town, we were-- I would say, we were about 100 families, Jewish families.
Now, it was a small town, like I said.
All the Jewish families lived, basically, in town, itself.
The-- the other ones, the gentiles, for instances, they lived more outside of the town.
It-- it wasn't a rich town.
There was no telephone in the town.
Not to talk about, there was no electricity.
Of course, radio was something strange to us.
We never heard about it.
So you could see, it was just a small, plain town.
But it was a lovely town.
The one thing where it was excellent in that town was the air and the water.
Because the food wasn't much-- What kind of food?
--because it was a small town.
Most of the food what I used to eat was vegetables.
That was-- whoever had a garden, you know, had vegetables and so on.
Meat, basically, we used to eat only for a Shabbos or a Yom Tov.
That was it.
No.
It was-- the closest, by we were, was, I would say, about 30 kilometers.
It was Tyachiv was the name of that city.
We used to go.
There was another town, a bigger town, where there used to be a market, a marketplace.
The marketplace used to be, I think, every month, used to be in a town.
It was-- the name was Teresva.
And as a matter of fact, my father sometimes used to go down there to see if he could do any business.
You know?
Well, to make some money there in the marketplace.
I had two aunts over there, with two uncles, a big family in that town, in Teresva.
So when your father would come back to town, after being in other countries, was he more--
My father used to come back.
We used to be so happy.
We were always used to wait, what he bought for us, as children.
I remember, once he came.
And he bought us, for me and my brothers, he bought us two sailor outfits.
You know, the shirt, with the pants, you know?
We were-- we were so happy about it, you know, that we have, for Yom Tov, you know, we'll going-- we're going to have the new outfits.
And of course, for our-- for our sisters, he got something and so on.
And so, basically, we were a very close family.
We used to live together, with my-- with my grandfather and grandmother.
We used to live together in-- it was--
On my mother's side, yeah.
On the father's side, they-- they lived a little bit further away.
But it wasn't much.
He tried.
He tried.
As a matter of fact, we had an uncle in the United States, on my-- on-- on my mother's side.
That was the brother of my mother.
And my father tried, you know?
We should-- we should immigrate to the United States.
He wrote a couple times to my uncle.
But the question always was-- the question always was, what-- what is my father going to do in the United States?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
When I was-- when I was 13-- when I was 13 years old, I had a bar mitzvah.
My father couldn't be at the bar mitzvah, because he was-- he was in Belgium at that time.
And he got sick.
A bar mitzvah, by us, was very simple.
You-- you went in the shul.
You said the haftoroh.
And then, you know, they invited the whole congregation to come home.
You had a schnapps.
You had a piece of cake.
And that was the whole deal of a bar mitzvah.
It wasn't like here, in the United States, where you spend all your fortunes on the bar mitzvah, where a child doesn't even know well to say the 'Baruch' and-- and after.
So then after that-- that was in August.
After that, my mother send me about 80-- 80 kilometers from our town, in a yeshiva.
The name of that town was Beregsaz, in Czechoslovakian, Berehovo
I was there a year, in that yeshiva.
After that, it started in 1938, '39, when Hitler marched into Czechoslovakia.
I came home.
And then-- and then our-- our-- our town-- not our town, but the whole-- whole part of that became Hungary.
The Hungarian took it over.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in 1941-- in 1941, they made a law that-- no.
In 1940, excuse me.
In 1940, they made a law that every Jew has to prove his birthplace, where he was born.

But unfortunately, those what couldn't-- what didn't-- what made a joke out of it, in 1941, one Saturday-- one Saturday, the only way you-- you could-- the only way you could-- you could bring the-- the town together, when you want to announce something, is there was a guy with a drum.
Like years ago, they used to say, hear ye, hear ye!
The same way.
He went with a drum.
And he called out that all the Jews, what cannot prove-- what cannot prove that they are born here as citizens, have to assemble tomorrow.
Unfortunately, my grandfather, but his grandfather was born in that town.
He had to go with-- with our grandmother.
My uncle, what he used to live next to us, he had eight children.
He was a butcher, by the way.
He had to go.
They all assembled.
They had all to assemble.
My-- my aunt and uncle in Teresva with eight children, they had to go.
So in other words, they took them away.
We didn't know where they're going to take them or for what.
They took them away to Galicia.
We never heard from them again.
And that's why I wonder, why I cannot hear or-- or read anything about it, what happened to those thousands of Jews from-- from the Carpathian.
That they took them away in 1941.
But I know one thing.
They took them to the Kamenets-Podolsk, in Galicia.
And-- and I would say, 98% of them never made it alive from there.
At that time I realized-- I started to realize what's going on.
But my father, he was in Germany when Hitler came to power.
He always told us what he saw, what they did in Germany.
So he was more afraid than we.
We didn't realize it yet.
We didn't realize what's going on.
That was in 1941.
Of course, they didn't take any Jews to the military.
But they took in the-- the Hungarians, that is.
They took in whoever was military-- military age.
They took them.
They called it munkatabor.
That means, in Hungarian, work camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they used to do, let's say, when the war started with Russia, they used to take those people, they used to get them on the Russian front, well, to dig trenches and so on.
Most of them never came back.
So I-- I've seen, already, what's-- what's going on there.
I went to Budapest, well, to look for some work.
My sister, Chia, the older sister, she went to Budapest.
She found some work there.
But I couldn't find any work.
And not long after that, I came back to Novoselica.
As I came back-- and, of course, I didn't go to school, because I wasn't allowed to go to school.
So they took me, also, what they called munkatabor, but not as-- as military, because I was still young.
There was a forest not far from us, where I had to go there and work.
We're cutting wood and put it on the train-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] to put it on the train and so on.
I was working there for over a year.
Of course, I never got paid for it.
The only thing, by the end of the week, what they used to give us, was flour and-- and-- should I-- and ham.
Of course, I'm religious.
We are religious at home.
We wouldn't use that.
What I used to do, I used to bring that ham to a gentile in a store.
He used to give me flour for the ham.
This is how-- this is how it was.
When that ended, I started to work.
There were one fellow, in the next town, in Neresnytsya they called it.
He was from Vienna.
When Hitler came to power, he ran away from Vienna.
He was a shoemaker.
I started to work for him, just to-- just to pass time.
Because I was bored.
I was at that age, you know?
I didn't know what to do.
In the meantime, my mother was home.
My father was home.
One sister was, by-- by the end, in Teresva.
So basically, it was my brother, Chaim, and Golda, what they were home.
That was up to 1944. 1944, when Hitler-- when Hitler took over Hungary, my-- that was before Pesach.
My father was very scared.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He told us, he says, I know what-- I know what Germans are.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Right the last day of Pesach, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the same fellow with the drum went out and he says, all the Jews have to assemble in the schoolyard.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] My father decided not to go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So my grandfather had land in-- in the mountains in our town.
At night, we took a few blankets, whatever food we had, we went there, up in the mountains, to hide.
I think there was a other couple families what went with us.
The next day, we were on the mountain.
And the next day, a fellow from our town, what we knew-- a gentile, of course-- he saw us there.
It didn't took long.
A fellow, by the name Antony-- they used to call him Anton-- his mother was German.
His father was from the Carpathians.
All of a sudden, I see him coming, with two guys-- actually, guys, they're the same age.
I used to go in school with them-- with guns.
As he saw us outside, he shot at us.
Probably he was a bad shot.
He didn't hurt anybody.
They took us under gun.
They took us down in town, to the schoolyard.
When we came to the schoolyard, there was already-- there was already a lot of Jews from our town in there.
Then I heard crying from the school, like children crying.
After that, I found out and I saw-- and I have seen, two little girls come out, their ears are bleeding.
That crying was they had earrings, golden earrings.
And this SOB wanted to take off the earrings.
This Anton want to take off the earrings.
He couldn't open them.
So he tore them down.
This is what that crying was all about.
Of course, we were so in a-- in a daze, we couldn't believe.
We couldn't believe that a thing like that would happen.
That we know the guy for so many years, that he would do a thing like that.
The next day, they took us to the next town, Neresnytsya.
We were there a couple days in the shul, in the synagogue.
Of course, there was no food.
Nobody gave us any food or whatever it is.
But we didn't even think about it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they put us on a train.
See, down there, it was a-- it was a small-- it was not the normal train.
It was a smaller version of a train.
Even the tracks were not as wide as the normal train is.
So they took us in an open car, in an open wagon, not-- not closed or whatever it is.
There are-- the-- it didn't took long.
We just had to go, I would say, about 20 kilometers, to the town of Teresva, or Teresva, how they used to call it.
Down there, the bigger, the normal car-- the normal train used to go from there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
As we were riding, we realized that we are-- we are riding more to inland of Hungary.
We came to a station by the name of Mateszalka.
That was the name of that town in-- in Hungary.
As we came down from the station, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were-- we were in open field for about two days.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they took us in-- in town, in the ghetto.
Now, the ghetto, the ghetto there was like this here.
They just told you, you're going to stay here.
It doesn't make any difference how many people are in that building.
So what happened?
It came out that we had to live in an attic, me, my father, Chia, my older daughter-- my older sister, Golda, and Chaim.
Also, my grandfather was there, Moshe.
First thing, what they-- what they did, they said everybody has to cut off his beard.
Now, telling-- telling for a person what's a 70-years-old, he never had a-- a razor-- a razor on his face.
That beard-- that beard is basically his face.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he didn't want to do it.
But my father convinced him.
My father knew what Germans are.
He convinced him to let shear off his-- his beard.
The first time when I've seen my grandfather without a beard.
And he looked at me.
We both started to cry.
It wasn't the same man.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In that ghetto was the first time that I've seen Germans.
There was one German, he was always drunk.
He was the most brutal man.
No reason at all, he'll walk by, he'll just hit you.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] As far as food is concerned, what they used to do, we used to go out, of course, under guard.
Because the way-- Mateszalka, I think, was not a poor town.
There was a lot of Jews there.
We used to have to collect food from there.
But we-- we couldn't use that food.
We had to steal food to bring it home, to-- to eat for our family.
This is how we survived Mateszalka.
One day, even still, and with all that-- that problem, they were still Jews, you know?
There was one fellow, a very religious Jew.
He had-- he made a cheder to teach children to read.
You know, and well, to learn.
All of a sudden, I see that German, that drunk went in there and took him out.
He beat him, mercifully.
It-- he made-- I have never seen what he did to-- well, to that poor old man.
I don't even know if that man ever survived that, that beating what he gave him.
Because he taught children.
This is how we went-- we were, there, in that ghetto, I would say, about a month.
We had to put it on a wagon, but no horses to drag it.
We had to schlep, ourselves, that wagons.
Because they-- they said that-- that horses are more important than we are.
